description: AEGIS self-scan scoping. AEGIS is a scanner CLI — console output IS the CLI's interface, scanner sources contain the detection patterns they match on as regex literals (structural self-match), and the repo is not a Next.js application. These suppressions ARE the user-facing answer to 'how do I scope AEGIS for a non-standard repo' — same knob real consumers get.

suppressions.reason: CLI package: console output IS the user interface. console.log/error/warn are intentional and map to stdout/stderr as required for a command-line tool, not debug artifacts.
Scanner source files log diagnostic output during scan orchestration (duration, progress, warnings). Not debug artifacts; not end-user-facing in a web context.

Scanner definition files contain the detection patterns (service_role, .rpc, supabase.method) as regex literals. A bare text-match gate will always self-match; AST-level analysis would be over-engineering for this class.
Taint-analyzer sinks and tracker reference supabase/rpc as detection targets in comments and literal arrays — not actual runtime Supabase client usage.
The crypto-auditor file defines the crypto-weakness detection patterns (md5, sha1, DES, JWT 'none'). Self-match is structural — these are PATTERNS-OF-CONCERN literals, not actual crypto usage.
Taint-tracker / function-summary match crypto primitive names (HMAC, digest, token-generation patterns) for taint-source / sanitizer bookkeeping. Not runtime crypto.

Supply-chain-scanner uses createHash for lockfile-integrity-hashing (content-fingerprint of package-lock.json / pnpm-lock.yaml), not for security-token generation. Pattern consistent with existing suppressions on scanners/ast and scanners/quality/* self-matches where crypto primitives serve as detection-infrastructure.
JWT-checker contains JWT algorithm names ('none', 'HS256', etc.) as detection literals. crypto-auditor matches the 'none' string as a structural FP on the detection source.
Scanner defining JWT-weakness detection references JWT algorithm literals. Self-match is structural.
sql-concat-checker defines SQL-injection detection patterns. The source literally contains .rpc() / .execute() template samples as documentation.

template-sql-checker defines SQL-injection detection patterns for the template-literal class. The source JSDoc documents the non-overlap contract with the older sql-concat-checker by literally citing db.query("SELECT ... " + id) as the string-concat shape owned by that sibling scanner. Structural self-match — same class as the existing sql-concat-checker.ts self-suppression, and the crypto-auditor / jwt-checker / xss-checker / redos-checker source self-matches documented above.
xss-checker defines XSS sink patterns (innerHTML, document.write, etc.) in its regex array. Structural self-match.
Taint-analyzer sinks / taint-tracker contain innerHTML / dangerouslySetInnerHTML as the detection targets. Same structural class.
ssrf-checker defines SSRF sink patterns (fetch / axios / got / url-parse references). Structural self-match.
Taint-tracker references fetch / axios as detection sinks in regex literals.

DAST probe modules are PURPOSE-BUILT to make HTTP calls to target URLs — that is their entire job (auth probe, privesc probe, rate-limit probe, race probe, header probe). The target URL is user-supplied at siege-mode time and INTENDED to be variable.
aegis fix posts to the user-configured AI-provider endpoint (--ai flag; defaults to local Ollama). The URL is variable by design.
aegis pentest targets the user-supplied URL against a running server for live DAST. Variable URL is the entire point of the command.
aegis siege orchestrates DAST probes against user-supplied targets. Variable URL is by design.
redos-checker contains nested-quantifier regex LITERALS used to DETECT catastrophic-backtracking patterns. The detection regex IS the thing redos-checker matches on.
logging-checker defines LOGGER_PACKAGES and LOG_CALL_PATTERNS as detection literals. Self-match is structural.
CLI package uses console as its control surface; no winston/pino needed. Scoped to avoid the prior blanket '**/*' suppression that taught users a bad template pattern (brutal-review M5).
Scanner package orchestrates scan runs via console; no centralized web-app logger applies. Scoped replacement for the prior blanket '**/*' suppression.
MCP server writes diagnostic JSON responses to stdio — structured logger does not apply to an MCP transport worker.
Reporter package emits formatted output (JSON / SARIF / terminal) as its primary function.
Core orchestration layer coordinates scanners and emits diagnostic stderr warnings; no structured web logger applies.
Lists security-header names (CSP, HSTS, etc.) as detection regex. Not a Next.js config file.
